---
title: "![](images/icons/icon_14.png){.title-icon} Regression"
subtitle: "Logistic, linear and conditional logistic regression - and when to use robust standard errors"
date: last-modified
---

::: {.callout-warning}
**Under development.** This page shows minimal, verified examples. More model types and diagnostics are coming.
:::

Most register analyses end in a regression model. Base R handles logistic (`glm`) and linear (`lm`) regression; matched case-control data require conditional logistic regression (`survival::clogit`). We use `gtsummary::tbl_regression()` to display the result neatly.

::: {.callout-note}
The code examples use generic path and variable names. Adapt them to your project. The packages used (`gtsummary`, `survival`, `sandwich`, `lmtest`) must be installed in your R environment on DST.
:::

---

## Starting point

```r
library(dplyr)
library(gtsummary)

df <- readRDS("path/to/analysis.rds")
```

## Logistic regression (binary outcome)

```r
model <- glm(outcome ~ exposure + age + sex, data = df, family = binomial)

model %>%
  tbl_regression(exponentiate = TRUE)   # exponentiate = TRUE gives odds ratios
```

- `family = binomial` makes it logistic regression.
- `exponentiate = TRUE` shows **odds ratios** (OR) instead of log-odds coefficients.

## Linear regression (continuous outcome)

```r
lm(bmi ~ exposure + age + sex, data = df) %>%
  tbl_regression()
```

## Matched case-control: conditional logistic regression

When you have matched in a nested case-control design ([Case-control](10b_case-control.qmd)), the analysis must respect the matching with **conditional** logistic regression. Use `clogit()` with a `strata()` term for the matched set.

```r
library(survival)

clogit(case ~ exposure + age + sex + strata(match_id), data = cc) %>%
  tbl_regression(exponentiate = TRUE)
```

- `strata(match_id)` tells the model which rows belong to the same matched set (`case.id` from `incidenceMatch()` or `Set` from `Epi::ccwc()`).
- `case` = 1 for a case, 0 for a control.

## Robust (clustered) standard errors {#klyngebaserede-standardfejl}

When the same person appears several times in the dataset - e.g. if the comparison cohort is matched **with replacement**, or a comparison person later becomes exposed (crossover, see [Comparison cohort](10a_sammenligningskohorte.qmd)) - the rows are not independent. If you ignore this, the confidence intervals come out too narrow. The fix is **clustered (robust) standard errors**, clustering on the person id.

For a `glm` model you compute a robust covariance matrix and test the coefficients with it:

```r
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)

model <- glm(outcome ~ exposure + age + sex, data = df, family = binomial)

coeftest(model, vcov = vcovCL(model, cluster = ~ pnr))
```

- `vcovCL(model, cluster = ~ pnr)` is the cluster-robust covariance matrix (clustered on `pnr`).
- `coeftest()` shows the coefficients with the robust standard errors.
- For a Cox model you specify it directly with the `cluster` argument in `coxph()` instead (coming on the time-to-event page).

## Check the model's assumptions (briefly)

- **Linear regression:** `plot(model)` gives the classic residual plots (linearity, homoscedasticity, normality of residuals).
- **Logistic/conditional logistic:** continuous variables are assumed linear in the log-odds. Check e.g. by modelling the variable flexibly (`splines::ns(age, 3)`) and comparing.
- **Proportional hazards** (for Cox models) belongs on the time-to-event page (`survival::cox.zph()`).

::: {.callout-note}
Remember: anything leaving DST must go through **output control** - no small cells, only aggregated results. See [Phase 14 - Export and repatriation](14_eksport-hjemsendelse.qmd).
:::
